_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against CDC STD Treatment Guidelines 2021, WHO Guidelines for the Treatment of Chlamydia trachomatis 2016, and peer-reviewed literature. All citations verified at source URLs.

h1Title: Chlamydia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, Treatment, and Prevention
heroOverview: Chlamydia is a sexually transmitted infection caused by the obligate intracellular bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis, serovars D-K, and is the most frequently reported bacterial sexually transmitted infection in the United States and Europe. It is frequently asymptomatic in both men and women — an estimated 70% of women and 50% of men with chlamydia develop no symptoms — which allows it to spread silently and cause serious reproductive complications before detection. In women, untreated chlamydia ascends the genital tract to cause pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), tubal scarring, ectopic pregnancy, and tubal factor infertility. In men, it causes epididymitis and, rarely, reactive arthritis. Effective single-dose and multi-dose antibiotic regimens cure chlamydia in over 95% of cases when taken correctly; partner treatment and retesting at 3 months are critical components of management.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1.6 million cases reported in the US in 2022 (CDC); estimated 4% of sexually active women aged 14-24 have chlamydia; true incidence is significantly higher due to underreporting and asymptomatic cases
keyStats.demographics: Women account for 75% of reported cases; highest rates in women aged 15-24; men who have sex with men (MSM) have elevated rates of extragenital chlamydia (rectal, pharyngeal)
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of diagnosis is 22 for women and 26 for men; most common in sexually active adolescents and young adults
keyStats.globalCases: WHO estimates 129 million new cases globally in 2020 in adults aged 15-49; the most common bacterial STI worldwide

definition: Chlamydia (ICD-10: A74.0 for chlamydial infection; N74.4 for female pelvic inflammatory disease due to chlamydia) is an infection of the mucous membranes of the genital tract, rectum, pharynx, or conjunctiva caused by Chlamydia trachomatis serovars D-K. The organism is an obligate intracellular bacterium that hijacks host cell machinery to replicate, forming intracytoplasmic inclusions visible on microscopy. It is transmitted through vaginal, anal, and oral sexual contact, and perinatally during delivery (causing neonatal conjunctivitis and chlamydial pneumonia). Chlamydia has a biphasic life cycle alternating between an infectious, metabolically inert elementary body and a metabolically active, replicating reticulate body. The infection is classified as uncomplicated (cervicitis, urethritis, proctitis) or complicated (PID, epididymitis, neonatal infection). It is reportable in all US states and most jurisdictions. Most people with chlamydia have no symptoms, making screening the primary public health tool for case identification and prevention of complications.

typesClassification.description: Infection limited to the urethra in men (urethritis) and/or the cervix in women (cervicitis). Presents with discharge, dysuria, or intermenstrual bleeding in some symptomatic individuals, but the majority are asymptomatic. Rectal chlamydia in MSM (from receptive anal intercourse) is frequently asymptomatic but may cause proctitis with rectal discharge, pain, or bleeding. Pharyngeal chlamydia is usually asymptomatic.

Ascending infection from the cervix to the endometrium (endometritis), fallopian tubes (salpingitis), and potentially the ovaries and pelvic peritoneum. Causes lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, adnexal tenderness, fever, and elevated inflammatory markers. Can be subclinical (silent PID). The leading cause of preventable tubal factor infertility worldwide.

Infection of the epididymis in men, typically unilateral scrotal pain, swelling, and tenderness radiating to the groin. In sexually active men under 35, chlamydia (and gonorrhea) is the most common cause. May be accompanied by urethritis symptoms. Can cause obstructive azoospermia if bilateral.

A sterile inflammatory arthritis triggered by chlamydial (and enteric) infection, presenting with the classic triad of arthritis, urethritis, and conjunctivitis. Chlamydia-triggered reactive arthritis is more common in men and is HLA-B27 associated. Can cause chronic erosive joint disease. Occurs in 1-3% of chlamydial infections.

Acquired perinatally during delivery through an infected birth canal. Chlamydial ophthalmia neonatorum (conjunctivitis) presents 5-14 days after birth with watery eye discharge and eyelid swelling; untreated it can cause corneal scarring and blindness. Chlamydial pneumonia presents at 4-12 weeks with staccato cough, tachypnea, and diffuse infiltrates on chest X-ray.

Caused by C. trachomatis serovars L1, L2, L3 — distinct from the STI serovars D-K. Characterized by a small painless genital ulcer at the site of inoculation (often unnoticed), followed 2-6 weeks later by painful inguinal lymphadenopathy (buboes) that may suppurate. More common in MSM; prevalent in Europe and North America since the 2000s. Requires longer antibiotic treatment than standard chlamydia.

primarySymptoms: Most people with urogenital chlamydia have NO symptoms — asymptomatic infection is the norm, particularly in women, making regular screening critical for sexually active individuals under 25 and others at risk.
Abnormal vaginal discharge — mucopurulent cervical discharge that may be yellow or cream-colored, often noted incidentally on pelvic examination as cervical friability and erythema.
Dysuria (painful urination) and urinary frequency in men with urethritis, or in women when urethral involvement co-occurs with cervicitis.
Intermenstrual bleeding or post-coital bleeding in women — a key clinical sign of cervicitis that should prompt testing.
Testicular pain, swelling, and tenderness in men with epididymitis — usually unilateral, associated with urethral discharge, and occurring in men under 35.
Lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, and adnexal tenderness in women — the clinical presentation of PID, indicating upper genital tract involvement.
Rectal discharge, anal pain, tenesmus, or bleeding in people with rectal chlamydia — most common in MSM but can occur in any person with receptive anal exposure.
Watery or purulent eye discharge in newborns, typically appearing 5-14 days after birth — chlamydial ophthalmia neonatorum, a preventable cause of blindness.
Conjunctivitis in adults following accidental ocular exposure to infected genital secretions — presents with redness, foreign body sensation, and watery discharge.
earlyWarningSigns: Any new abnormal discharge from the vagina, penis, or rectum in a sexually active person — even if mild
Pain or burning with urination that is new or worsening
Bleeding between periods or after intercourse in women — a red flag for cervicitis
Unilateral testicular pain and swelling in a sexually active man under 40 — consider epididymitis
Persistent lower abdominal pain in women, especially with fever or dyspareunia — possible PID
Joint pain, particularly in the knees and ankles, occurring 1-4 weeks after a sexually transmitted infection — consider reactive arthritis
emergencySigns: Severe lower abdominal pain with high fever (>38.5C), chills, and nausea in women — possible tubo-ovarian abscess or severe PID requiring emergent hospitalization and IV antibiotics
Signs of ectopic pregnancy (abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, syncope) in a woman with known or suspected chlamydia — a medical emergency; ectopic rupture is life-threatening
Neonatal conjunctivitis with marked eyelid swelling and purulent discharge within the first 2 weeks of life — requires immediate evaluation to prevent corneal scarring and blindness
Severe scrotal pain with high fever in a young man — consider epididymo-orchitis complicated by abscess
Neurological symptoms (meningismus, headache, photophobia) in a patient with disseminated LGV — rare but serious complication

causes.cause: Unprotected sexual contact with an infected partner
causes.description: Chlamydia is transmitted through vaginal, anal, and oral sexual contact with an infected partner. The risk of transmission from a single act of unprotected vaginal intercourse with an infected partner is approximately 10-30% for women and 5-25% for men, depending on the study. Condoms reduce transmission risk by approximately 90% when used consistently and correctly.

causes.cause: Asymptomatic infection in the transmitting partner
causes.description: The majority of chlamydial infections are asymptomatic — most people who transmit chlamydia do not know they are infected. This explains the high community prevalence and why screening is essential even when partners appear healthy. Asymptomatic carriers shed infectious elementary bodies intermittently for months to years without treatment.

Inadequate treatment (wrong antibiotic, dose, or duration), or failure to complete a prescribed course, fails to eradicate the organism. Repeat infections are also common — approximately 5-10% of treated patients are reinfected within 3-6 months, typically from an untreated sexual partner. Repeat infections significantly increase the risk of PID and infertility in women.

causes.cause: Vertical transmission from mother to neonate
causes.description: An estimated 30-40% of infants born vaginally to mothers with active chlamydial cervicitis acquire the infection during passage through the birth canal. Cesarean delivery does not prevent transmission if membranes are ruptured. Perinatal transmission causes chlamydial ophthalmia neonatorum in 20-50% of exposed infants and chlamydial pneumonia in 10-20%.

Co-infection with gonorrhea is common (10-30% of gonorrhea cases also have chlamydia) due to shared risk behaviors. Co-infection with HIV increases susceptibility to chlamydia and may increase the risk of chlamydial complications. Herpes simplex virus and trichomoniasis co-infections are also documented.

Without effective partner notification and expedited partner therapy (EPT) — the practice of prescribing antibiotics for the sexual partner without a clinical evaluation — infected partners remain untreated, reinfect the original patient, and continue spreading infection in the community. This is a major public health challenge.

riskFactors.description: Young age is the single strongest risk factor for chlamydia — the cervical ectopy (immature transformation zone) in adolescents provides a larger surface area vulnerable to chlamydial infection. CDC recommends annual chlamydia screening for all sexually active women under 25.
Each new sexual partner in the past 60 days increases exposure risk. Having two or more partners in the past year increases chlamydia risk 5-fold compared to monogamous individuals.

riskFactors.factor: Previous chlamydia or other STI infection
riskFactors.description: A prior chlamydia infection confers partial but incomplete immunity — reinfection rates are 5-10% within 3-6 months of treatment. A history of any STI indicates both behavioral risk and a potentially compromised mucosal barrier.

Condoms reduce chlamydia transmission by approximately 90% with consistent use. Inconsistent use (using condoms only sometimes) provides minimal protection. Unprotected receptive anal intercourse carries the highest per-act risk.

riskFactors.factor: Men who have sex with men (MSM)
riskFactors.description: MSM have elevated rates of chlamydia at all anatomic sites (urethral, rectal, pharyngeal). Rectal chlamydia in MSM is frequently asymptomatic and a major reservoir for transmission. CDC recommends annual rectal and urethral screening for sexually active MSM.

Gonorrhea and chlamydia share risk factors and frequently co-occur — approximately 10-30% of people with gonorrhea also have chlamydia. All patients diagnosed with gonorrhea should be presumptively treated for chlamydia and tested.

riskFactors.factor: Substance use linked to risky sexual behavior
riskFactors.description: Alcohol and drug use before sex reduces condom use and increases the likelihood of multiple partners. Methamphetamine use in particular is strongly associated with high-risk sexual networks and chlamydia outbreaks.

Sexual assault and coercion are associated with increased risk of STIs including chlamydia, both from the assault itself and from subsequent behavioral and biological changes. STI testing should be offered after sexual assault.

affectedDemographics: Chlamydia is the most commonly reported notifiable disease in the United States — over 1.6 million cases were reported to the CDC in 2022, though true incidence is estimated to be 2-3 times higher due to underdiagnosis and asymptomatic cases
Women account for approximately 75% of reported cases, partly due to more aggressive screening programs and partly because women experience more complications, increasing detection
The highest age-specific rates occur in women aged 15-24 — approximately 4% of sexually active women in this age group have chlamydia

Black and African American individuals have rates of chlamydia 5-8 times higher than white individuals in the US — reflecting structural factors including reduced healthcare access, poverty, and stigma rather than biological differences
MSM have elevated rates of rectal chlamydia (up to 10-15% in some urban clinic populations) and are at risk for both urogenital and extragenital infection
Adolescents and young adults (ages 15-24) account for approximately 70% of all reported chlamydia cases despite representing only 25% of sexually active individuals
diagnosisOverview: Chlamydia is diagnosed through nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT), which detects chlamydial DNA or RNA in endocervical, vaginal, urine, urethral, rectal, or pharyngeal specimens. NAAT is the most sensitive test available (sensitivity 90-95%) and can be performed on first-catch urine (avoiding the need for a speculum exam in women), self-collected vaginal swabs, or provider-collected endocervical specimens. In men, a urethral swab or first-catch urine specimen is used. For MSM, rectal and pharyngeal NAAT should be performed as these sites are not captured by urogenital testing. NAAT is more sensitive than culture (which requires special lab handling and is less sensitive) or antigen detection methods. Urine testing is equally sensitive to cervical/urethral swab testing in both symptomatic and asymptomatic individuals. Point-of-care rapid NAAT tests with same-day results are increasingly available in sexual health clinics. HIV testing should be offered to all patients with chlamydia, and testing for other STIs (gonorrhea, syphilis, trichomonas) is standard.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard diagnostic test for urogenital chlamydia in men and women. Detects chlamydial DNA or rRNA in urine, urethral, endocervical, or vaginal specimens with sensitivity of 90-95% and specificity of 99%.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: For urine testing: first-catch urine (first 20-30 mL of the stream, after not urinating for at least 1 hour). For cervical/vaginal testing: provider-collected endocervical swab or self-collected vaginal swab. Results in 1-7 days depending on the laboratory. Point-of-care NAAT gives results within 90 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screening for extragenital chlamydia in MSM and others with receptive anal or oral sexual exposure. Urogenital testing alone misses 70-90% of chlamydial infections in MSM.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Rectal swab: self-collected or provider-collected from the anal canal. Pharyngeal swab: provider-collected from the tonsillar fossae and posterior pharynx. Both are well-tolerated. Not FDA-cleared for these sites in all labs (research use / laboratory-developed tests), but CDC supports testing these sites.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Simultaneous detection of Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae from the same specimen, improving efficiency. Most NAAT platforms offer dual testing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same specimen collection as for chlamydia alone. Results typically available within 1-7 days. Positive for chlamydia, gonorrhea, both, or neither.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Standard of care for all patients diagnosed with chlamydia, given shared risk factors. HIV risk is increased in the presence of chlamydia (particularly rectal inflammation) and vice versa.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for HIV antibody/antigen combo test and syphilis serology (RPR or VDRL). Results in 1-7 days for conventional testing, or 20-30 minutes for rapid point-of-care HIV testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: In symptomatic men and women, Gram stain of urethral or cervical discharge is performed to look for evidence of urethritis (polymorphonuclear leukocytes) and to identify gonorrhea (Gram-negative intracellular diplococci). NAAT follows to confirm chlamydia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Urethral swab for Gram stain and NAAT; cervical swab for NAAT. Gram stain results within minutes. Culture for gonorrhea may be sent simultaneously for antibiotic susceptibility testing in high-prevalence areas.

diagnosticTests.purpose: CDC recommends retesting all chlamydia patients approximately 3 months after treatment to detect reinfection (from untreated partners) or treatment failure. This is standard of care, not an indicator of treatment failure.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same NAAT testing as the initial diagnosis, using urine or swabs. Can be done at a follow-up clinic visit or via mail-in self-collection kit.

treatmentOverview: Chlamydia is treated with antibiotics. The CDC 2021 STD Treatment Guidelines recommend doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days as the preferred regimen for all chlamydial infections, including urogenital, rectal, and pharyngeal sites — based on superior microbiological cure rates compared to azithromycin (95% vs 85% for rectal chlamydia). Alternative regimens include azithromycin 1 g orally as a single dose (convenient for single-dose observed therapy but with slightly lower efficacy, particularly for rectal infection) and levofloxacin 500 mg orally once daily for 7 days (second-line, for patients who cannot tolerate doxycycline or azithromycin). PID requires more aggressive treatment: ceftriaxone 1 g IM/IV daily plus doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily, with or without metronidazole, for 14 days, often requiring hospitalization for IV antibiotics in moderate-to-severe cases. Epididymitis is treated with ceftriaxone 500 mg IM as a single dose plus doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 10 days. Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT) — providing prescriptions or medications for sexual partners without requiring a clinical evaluation — is a legal and recommended public health intervention in most US states to prevent reinfection. All sexual partners within the past 60 days should be evaluated and presumptively treated.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days
medicalTreatments.description: First-line CDC-recommended treatment for all chlamydial infections at any anatomic site. Achieves microbiological cure rates of 95-98%. Contraindicated in pregnancy (causes fetal harm) and in children under 8 years old (tooth discoloration). Must not be taken with dairy products or antacids, which chelate the drug and reduce absorption. Photosensitivity is a common side effect — sunscreen is essential. Esophageal irritation can be avoided by taking with a full glass of water and remaining upright for 30 minutes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Microbiological cure rate of 95-98% for urogenital and rectal chlamydia. Superior to azithromycin for rectal chlamydia in MSM. Now preferred over azithromycin for all sites based on 2021 CDC guidelines.

medicalTreatments.name: Azithromycin 1 g orally as a single dose (Zithromax Z-Pack)
medicalTreatments.description: Single-dose alternative to doxycycline — convenient for patients who may not return for follow-up or where observed treatment is desired. Well tolerated. Less effective than doxycycline for rectal chlamydia (cure rate approximately 85-90% vs 95%+). Safe in pregnancy (Category B). Single dose minimizes compliance issues.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Microbiological cure rate approximately 90-95% for urogenital chlamydia, approximately 85% for rectal chlamydia. Lower efficacy than doxycycline for rectal infection is the primary limitation.

medicalTreatments.name: Levofloxacin 500 mg orally once daily for 7 days
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line alternative for patients who cannot tolerate doxycycline or azithromycin. Fluoroquinolone — avoid in patients with history of tendon disorders or on concurrent corticosteroids. Not recommended as first-line due to side effect profile and rising fluoroquinolone resistance in general.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Microbiological cure rate approximately 90-95% for urogenital chlamydia.

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline (for PID or epididymitis)
medicalTreatments.description: For complicated chlamydia (PID, epididymitis), ceftriaxone IM/IV (covers gonorrhea co-infection) combined with doxycycline orally for 14 days is standard. Metronidazole is often added for PID to cover anaerobes and bacterial vaginosis-associated organisms. Moderate-to-severe PID requires hospitalization for IV ceftriaxone and doxycycline.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure rates for PID exceed 85% with appropriate antibiotic regimens. Prevention of long-term sequelae (infertility, ectopic pregnancy) depends on early treatment before irreversible tubal damage occurs.

medicalTreatments.name: Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT)
medicalTreatments.description: Prescribing or providing antibiotics for the sexual partner(s) of a patient with chlamydia without requiring a separate clinical evaluation of the partner. Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days is the recommended EPT regimen. EPT is legally authorized in most US states and recommended by CDC when a partner cannot or will not seek care.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces reinfection rates significantly. A randomized controlled trial showed a 50% reduction in reinfection rates when EPT was offered compared to patient referral alone.

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopy for diagnosis and treatment of PID
surgicalOptions.description: Gold standard for diagnosing PID (allowing direct visualization of the fallopian tubes and ovaries), but typically reserved for cases where diagnosis is uncertain or when pelvic abscess requires drainage. Not a first-line treatment for uncomplicated chlamydia or PID.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Diagnostic accuracy near 100% for tubal inflammation and adhesions. Therapeutic benefit is primarily from washout of purulent material and adhesiolysis in severe cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Salpingectomy or salpingostomy for ectopic pregnancy
surgicalOptions.description: When chlamydial PID causes tubal scarring leading to ectopic pregnancy, surgical management (salpingectomy — removal of the affected tube) or medical management (methotrexate) may be required depending on clinical criteria. This is a consequence of inadequately treated chlamydia, not a primary treatment for chlamydia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: For unruptured ectopic pregnancy, methotrexate achieves complete resolution in approximately 65-95% of cases. Salpingectomy is definitive for the acute ectopic crisis.

surgicalOptions.name: Neonatal chlamydial pneumonia — ICU-level supportive care
surgicalOptions.description: Severe neonatal chlamydial pneumonia, particularly in preterm infants, may require oxygen support, mechanical ventilation, and nasogastric feeding. Systemic erythromycin (50 mg/kg/day in four divided doses for 14 days) is the treatment.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Erythromycin achieves clinical resolution in approximately 80% of neonatal chlamydial pneumonia. Failure to treat risks chronic lung disease and Bronchopulmonary Dysplasia.

alternativeTreatments.name: None for active chlamydia infection
alternativeTreatments.description: There are no proven effective non-antibiotic treatments for chlamydia. Complementary therapies, herbal remedies (echinacea, garlic, goldenseal), probiotics, and dietary interventions have no role in treating active chlamydial infection. These should not be used as alternatives to evidence-based antibiotic treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Post-treatment probiotic support
alternativeTreatments.description: Probiotics (Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG, Lactobacillus reuteri) may help restore normal vaginal and gut microbiota disrupted by antibiotics. Limited evidence for reducing antibiotic-associated diarrhea. Not a treatment for chlamydia itself.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Probiotics modestly reduce the risk of antibiotic-associated diarrhea and vaginal candidiasis following doxycycline or azithromycin treatment. No evidence they affect chlamydia treatment outcomes.

whySeeSpecialist: A sexual health specialist, infectious disease physician, or gynecologist/urologist should be seen when chlamydia is diagnosed in a pregnant patient (to prevent perinatal transmission); when PID is suspected (upper abdominal pain, fever, cervical motion tenderness, or signs of tubo-ovarian abscess); when a patient fails standard antibiotic treatment (persistent positive NAAT at 3-4 weeks after completing treatment); when co-infection with HIV, syphilis, or gonorrhea is present; when LGV is suspected (inguinal lymphadenopathy, MSM); when chlamydia causes epididymitis; or when recurrent chlamydia infections indicate a network-level transmission problem requiring partner notification support. Complex or treatment-resistant cases, and all cases of chlamydia in children, warrant specialist evaluation.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a clinician with specific expertise in sexual health — not all primary care physicians are comfortable with or knowledgeable about the nuances of STI management. A sexual health clinic, Planned Parenthood, Title X family planning clinic, or infectious disease specialist typically has the most experience with chlamydia management. The clinician should offer partner notification support, EPT where legally available, retesting at 3 months, and screening for co-occurring STIs including HIV.
preventionStrategies: Use condoms consistently and correctly for every act of vaginal, anal, and oral sex — latex or polyurethane condoms reduce transmission risk by approximately 90%
Get screened annually for chlamydia if you are a sexually active woman under 25, or at any age if you have new or multiple sexual partners
MSM should be screened for rectal and urethral chlamydia at least annually — more frequently (every 3-6 months) if multiple partners
Discuss STI status with sexual partners before engaging in sexual activity — open communication about testing and status is a form of risk reduction
Limit the number of sexual partners — each new partner increases exposure risk
Seek testing promptly after any unprotected sexual contact with a partner of unknown status
If you are diagnosed with chlamydia, complete all prescribed treatment and ensure your partner(s) are treated before resuming sexual activity
lifestyleModifications: Abstain from all sexual activity (vaginal, anal, oral) for 7 days after completing single-dose azithromycin treatment, or until doxycycline course is completed, and until all sexual partners have been treated — to prevent reinfection
Inform all sexual partners from the past 60 days about the chlamydia diagnosis so they can be tested and treated
Avoid alcohol during PID treatment — metronidazole (often used in PID regimens) interacts with alcohol and causes disulfiram-like reactions
Use barrier protection (condoms) consistently during all sexual activity to prevent reinfection and new infections
Keep follow-up appointments — CDC recommends retesting at 3 months post-treatment regardless of symptoms to detect reinfection early

recommended: Water — staying well-hydrated supports the body's inflammatory response and helps flush bacteria from the urinary tract during treatment
Probiotic-rich foods (yogurt, kefir, fermented vegetables) — restore normal gut flora disrupted by antibiotics and reduce the risk of antibiotic-associated diarrhea and vaginal yeast infection
High-protein foods (lean meats, fish, legumes) — support immune function and tissue repair during infection
Fruits and vegetables rich in vitamin C (citrus, bell peppers, broccoli) — vitamin C supports immune function and tissue healing
Whole grains — provide sustained energy and fiber to support overall health during and after treatment
avoid: Dairy products and calcium supplements when taking doxycycline — calcium chelates doxycycline and significantly reduces its absorption, making the antibiotic less effective
Alcohol — particularly important during PID treatment when metronidazole is used; alcohol causes severe nausea, vomiting, and flushing with metronidazole
Spicy or acidic foods if experiencing urethritis/cervicitis symptoms — may irritate the inflamed mucosa and worsen discomfort
Caffeine — may worsen dysuria and urinary frequency associated with urethritis; moderate intake during treatment
Processed foods and excessive sugar — these suppress immune function and can promote candida overgrowth during antibiotic treatment

exerciseGuidelines: During treatment for uncomplicated chlamydia, moderate exercise is safe and may support immune function through improved circulation and stress reduction. However, avoid intense or strenuous exercise if you have fever, pelvic pain (suggestive of PID), or epididymitis, as these indicate the infection has spread and the body needs rest. Walking is safe in most cases. For PID patients, bed rest may be recommended acutely. Swimming should be avoided until treatment is complete and symptoms have fully resolved to prevent introducing additional pathogens. Once fully recovered (usually 1-2 weeks after completing antibiotics), all normal exercise can be resumed without restriction.
dailyManagement: Take antibiotics exactly as prescribed — complete the full course even if symptoms resolve before finishing, and do not skip doses

If prescribed doxycycline: take with food and a full glass of water; stay upright for 30 minutes after taking to prevent esophageal irritation; apply sunscreen daily as photosensitivity is significant
Abstain from sexual activity until both you and your partner(s) have completed treatment (7 days after a single dose, or after completing a multi-day course)
Schedule the recommended retest at 3 months post-treatment — this is standard of care, not optional
Contact all sexual partners from the past 60 days to inform them of the diagnosis and need for testing
If symptoms worsen during treatment (fever, worsening pain, new symptoms), contact a clinician immediately — possible treatment failure or complications
prognosis: With appropriate antibiotic treatment, chlamydia is curable in over 95% of cases. Complications from chlamydia — including PID, tubal scarring, infertility, ectopic pregnancy, chronic pelvic pain, and epididymitis — are almost entirely preventable with early detection and timely treatment. The greatest threat to a good outcome is delayed diagnosis and treatment, typically due to asymptomatic infection. Untreated chlamydia persists for months to years and causes progressive reproductive damage in women — the risk of infertility increases with duration of untreated infection. Recurrent infections (reinfection from untreated partners) are common and compound the risk of tubal damage. With early treatment, before the infection ascends beyond the cervix, fertility is fully preserved. Partner treatment is essential to prevent ping-pong reinfection and community transmission.
recoveryTimeline: With antibiotic treatment: symptom improvement begins within 48-72 hours for most patients with uncomplicated chlamydia. Discharge and dysuria typically resolve within 1-2 weeks. Complete microbiological cure is achieved by the end of the treatment course (for azithromycin) or within 1-2 weeks after completing doxycycline. PID treatment takes longer — 14 days minimum, with symptoms improving over 48-72 hours; full resolution may take 2-4 weeks. Retesting at 3 months is mandatory to detect reinfection. Epididymitis symptoms improve within 1-3 days of starting antibiotics, with full resolution in 2-4 weeks. Neonatal chlamydial conjunctivitis and pneumonia treated with systemic erythromycin: conjunctivitis resolves within 2-5 days; pneumonia may take 2-4 weeks for full radiographic clearance.
complications: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID) — ascending chlamydial infection causes endometritis and salpingitis in 10-20% of infected women, which can cause tubal scarring, adhesions, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility even after the infection is treated

Tubal factor infertility — chlamydial PID is the leading cause of preventable infertility worldwide; the risk of infertility after a single episode of PID is approximately 8-10%, rising to 20-40% after three or more episodes
Ectopic pregnancy — tubal scarring from chlamydial PID increases the risk of ectopic pregnancy 6-10-fold; approximately 10% of all ectopic pregnancies are attributable to chlamydial infection
Chronic pelvic pain — tubal and peritoneal adhesions resulting from PID cause chronic pain that persists long after the infection is eradicated
Epididymitis — untreated chlamydial urethritis can ascend to cause epididymitis in men, potentially causing obstructive azoospermia if bilateral
Reactive arthritis — a sterile inflammatory arthritis triggered by chlamydia, causing joint pain, urethritis, and conjunctivitis; can become chronic and erosive
Neonatal conjunctivitis and pneumonia — perinatally acquired chlamydia causes preventable ocular and pulmonary disease in infants, including risk of blindness from corneal scarring
Increased HIV susceptibility — chlamydial infection, particularly rectal inflammation, increases the risk of HIV acquisition by 2-5 fold through mucosal immune disruption

supportResources.name: CDC STD Treatment Guidelines 2021 — Chlamydia
supportResources.description: The definitive US clinical guideline for chlamydia diagnosis and treatment, including PID management, partner notification, and special populations. Updated 2021 with doxycycline as preferred regimen.

supportResources.name: WHO Guidelines for the Treatment of Chlamydia trachomatis. World Health Organization; 2016.
supportResources.description: Global treatment guidelines from WHO covering all aspects of chlamydia management in resource-limited and well-resourced settings.

supportResources.name: Planned Parenthood — Chlamydia Information
supportResources.description: Patient-friendly information on chlamydia symptoms, testing, treatment, and prevention. Includes a clinic finder for affordable testing.

supportResources.name: CDC National Notifiable Diseases Surveillance System — Chlamydia
supportResources.description: Annual US surveillance data for chlamydia, gonorrhea, and syphilis — the primary epidemiological resource for understanding disease burden and trends.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gonorrhea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gonorrhea is caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae, a Gram-negative diplococcus, while chlamydia is caused by Chlamydia trachomatis, an obligate intracellular bacterium. Both cause urethritis and cervicitis and frequently co-occur, but gonorrhea is more likely to cause purulent discharge and symptomatic infection. Gram stain of urethral discharge shows intracellular Gram-negative diplococci in gonorrhea but not in chlamydia. NAAT is required for definitive diagnosis of both and they are typically tested for simultaneously.

confusedWithConditions.name: Urinary Tract Infection (UTI)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: UTIs cause dysuria and urinary frequency in both men and women, similar to chlamydial urethritis. However, UTIs typically present with suprapubic pain, hematuria, and positive nitrites or leukocyte esterase on urinalysis, and are caused by uropathogenic E. coli rather than C. trachomatis. Chlamydia urethritis in men may be misdiagnosed as UTI. A sexual history and NAAT for chlamydia are indicated in sexually active individuals with dysuria.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bacterial Vaginosis (BV)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: BV causes vaginal discharge and odor but is not sexually transmitted and is caused by an overgrowth of anaerobic bacteria (Gardnerella vaginalis, Prevotella, Mobiluncus) disrupting normal Lactobacillus-dominant vaginal flora. Chlamydia causes cervicitis with mucopurulent discharge and cervical friability. The two can co-occur. BV does not cause infertility or PID, unlike chlamydia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Trichomoniasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Trichomoniasis is caused by Trichomonas vaginalis, a flagellated protozoan parasite. It causes frothy, malodorous vaginal discharge and vulvovaginal irritation in women, and urethritis in men. Trichomonas is diagnosed by wet mount microscopy (motile trichomonads), NAAT, or rapid antigen test. Chlamydia is a bacterial infection diagnosed by NAAT. The two are unrelated pathogens with different treatments.

relatedConditions.name: Chlamydia (Urogenital)
relatedConditions.relevance: This condition page focuses on urogenital chlamydia caused by C. trachomatis serovars D-K — the most common chlamydial STI.

relatedConditions.name: Gonorrhea
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently co-occurs with chlamydia — 10-30% of gonorrhea cases also involve chlamydia. All patients with one should be tested for the other. Shared risk factors and transmission dynamics.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: STIs including chlamydia increase HIV acquisition risk 2-5 fold. HIV-positive individuals with chlamydia may have higher bacterial loads. Bidirectional screening is standard of care.

relatedConditions.name: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: The most serious complication of untreated chlamydia in women — ascending infection causes PID, which can lead to infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain.

relatedConditions.name: Trichomoniasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another common STI with overlapping risk factors. Often tested for simultaneously. Distinct pathogen with different treatment.

faqs.question: What is chlamydia and how do you get it?
faqs.answer: Chlamydia is a sexually transmitted infection caused by the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis (serovars D-K). It is transmitted through vaginal, anal, and oral sexual contact with an infected partner — even if that partner has no symptoms. It can also be passed from an infected mother to her baby during vaginal delivery, causing eye infection and pneumonia in the newborn. You cannot get chlamydia from toilet seats, swimming pools, or casual physical contact.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of chlamydia?
faqs.answer: Most people with chlamydia have no symptoms at all — this is the most important thing to know. When symptoms do occur, they may include: abnormal vaginal or penile discharge, pain or burning with urination, pain during intercourse, bleeding between periods or after sex (in women), testicular pain (in men), and lower abdominal pain (if PID has developed). Rectal chlamydia may cause discharge, pain, or bleeding from the rectum. Any new symptoms after sexual contact should prompt testing.

faqs.question: How do you test for chlamydia?
faqs.answer: Chlamydia is tested using a nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT), which detects the genetic material of the bacteria. In women, it can be done on a urine sample, a self-collected vaginal swab, or a swab taken by a clinician from the cervix. In men, it is done on a urine sample or a urethral swab. For men who have sex with men, rectal and throat swabs should also be tested as these are common sites of infection not detected by urine testing alone. The test is painless (urine or swab-based) and results are available within 1-7 days.

faqs.question: How is chlamydia treated?
faqs.answer: Chlamydia is treated with antibiotics. The CDC's preferred regimen is doxycycline 100 mg taken by mouth twice daily for 7 days — this cure rate is over 95%. An alternative is azithromycin 1 g as a single dose, which is convenient but slightly less effective, especially for rectal infection. Levofloxacin is a second-line option for people who cannot take doxycycline or azithromycin. All treatments require a doctor's prescription. It is essential that sexual partners are also treated to prevent reinfection.

faqs.question: Can chlamydia be cured?
faqs.answer: Yes — chlamydia is curable with a complete course of antibiotics. The cure rate with doxycycline is over 95%. Treatment failure is rare and is usually due to not completing the medication, reinfection from an untreated partner, or (very rarely) antibiotic resistance. After completing treatment and waiting the recommended period (7 days after a single dose or after finishing a multi-day course), you should no longer be infectious. A retest at 3 months is recommended to confirm the infection has been cleared and to check for reinfection.

faqs.question: Can I get chlamydia again after being treated?
faqs.answer: Yes — having chlamydia once does not make you immune. Reinfection is common, occurring in approximately 5-10% of treated patients within 3-6 months, almost always from an untreated sexual partner. This is why partner treatment is absolutely essential. Even if you completed your antibiotics, if your partner was not treated, they can reinfect you. Retesting at 3 months after treatment is recommended to detect any reinfection early. With each repeat infection, the risk of complications (PID, infertility) increases.

faqs.question: Can chlamydia affect my fertility?
faqs.answer: Yes — untreated chlamydia is a leading cause of preventable infertility, particularly in women. The bacteria ascend from the cervix into the uterus and fallopian tubes, causing inflammation and scarring (pelvic inflammatory disease, PID). Scarring of the fallopian tubes can block the passage of eggs and sperm, preventing fertilization, or create conditions for ectopic pregnancy (a pregnancy implanted in the tube rather than the uterus). Approximately 10-20% of women with untreated chlamydia develop PID, and 8-10% of those will become infertile after a single episode. This is why early detection and treatment — before the infection ascends — preserves fertility.

faqs.question: Should my partner be treated for chlamydia?
faqs.answer: Absolutely — all sexual partners from the past 60 days should be notified, evaluated, and presumptively treated. This is critical both for the partner's health and to prevent reinfection of the original patient. Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT) — where your clinician provides antibiotics for your partner without examining them — is recommended in most US states and is a CDC-endorsed strategy. Your partner does not need to have symptoms to have chlamydia; most people with chlamydia have no symptoms. Resuming sexual activity before both partners are treated guarantees reinfection.

faqs.question: Can I have chlamydia while pregnant?
faqs.answer: Yes — chlamydia can occur during pregnancy and is associated with serious risks including miscarriage, preterm birth, low birth weight, and perinatal transmission to the baby. Pregnant women diagnosed with chlamydia should be treated with azithromycin (doxycycline is contraindicated in pregnancy). Chlamydia can be passed to the baby during vaginal delivery, causing eye infection (conjunctivitis) and potentially pneumonia. All pregnant women should be screened for chlamydia at their first prenatal visit, and retested in the third trimester if under 25 or at high risk.

faqs.question: Does doxycycline treat rectal and pharyngeal chlamydia?
faqs.answer: Yes — doxycycline is now the preferred treatment for chlamydia at all anatomic sites, including rectal and pharyngeal infections. This is a change from earlier guidelines that recommended azithromycin for pharyngeal infection. The 2021 CDC guidelines preferentially recommend doxycycline for all sites because it achieves higher microbiological cure rates, particularly for rectal chlamydia (95%+ vs 85% with azithromycin). Pharyngeal chlamydia is less common and is usually asymptomatic but should still be treated.

faqs.question: What happens if chlamydia is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated chlamydia can spread and cause serious complications over weeks to months. In women: cervicitis progresses to pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), which can cause tubal scarring, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, and ectopic pregnancy. In men: it can cause epididymitis (painful swelling of the epididymis) and, rarely, reactive arthritis. Both men and women can develop reactive arthritis (arthritis, urethritis, conjunctivitis). Pregnant women can pass it to their baby during delivery. The longer chlamydia goes untreated, the greater the risk of these complications.

faqs.question: Who should be screened for chlamydia?
faqs.answer: CDC recommends annual chlamydia screening for: all sexually active women under 25; all pregnant women at their first prenatal visit (and again in the third trimester if under 25 or high risk); women with new or multiple sexual partners; MSM (annually for urethral and rectal sites, more often if high-risk); and anyone with a recent chlamydia or gonorrhea infection. If you have any symptoms (discharge, pain, bleeding), get tested regardless of age or risk factors. Testing is simple (usually a urine test), painless, and widely available.

faqs.question: Can chlamydia cause infertility in men?
faqs.answer: While chlamydia-related infertility is far more common and better documented in women, chlamydia can affect male fertility. Chlamydial epididymitis can cause scarring in the epididymis and, if bilateral, obstructive azoospermia (blockage of sperm delivery). Additionally, chlamydia can directly affect sperm quality — studies show chlamydia infection is associated with reduced sperm count, motility, and increased DNA fragmentation in sperm. Men with chlamydia should be treated promptly.

faqs.question: What is the difference between chlamydia and gonorrhea?
faqs.answer: Chlamydia and gonorrhea are both sexually transmitted bacterial infections that cause similar symptoms (urethritis, cervicitis, discharge) and frequently occur together. However, they are caused by different bacteria — Chlamydia trachomatis (an intracellular bacterium) vs. Neisseria gonorrhoeae (a Gram-negative diplococcus visible on microscopy). Gonorrhea is more likely to cause obvious purulent discharge and symptomatic infection. Both are diagnosed by NAAT and both can cause PID and infertility in women and epididymitis in men. Treatment is different: chlamydia is treated with doxycycline or azithromycin, while gonorrhea requires ceftriaxone (a cephalosporin injection). CDC recommends testing for both simultaneously whenever one is suspected.

faqs.question: How long after treatment can I have sex again?
faqs.answer: You should abstain from all sexual activity (vaginal, anal, oral) until both you and your partner(s) have completed treatment. For azithromycin (single dose), this means waiting 7 days after taking the dose. For doxycycline (7-day course), wait until the full course is completed and 7 days have passed since the last dose. If you had PID or epididymitis, you may need to wait longer — follow your clinician's guidance. Resuming sexual activity before your partner is treated will almost certainly result in reinfection.

faqs.question: Is chlamydia related to trachoma?
faqs.answer: Yes — both trachoma and genital chlamydia are caused by different strains (serovars) of the same species, Chlamydia trachomatis. Serovars A-C cause trachoma, a devastating eye infection that is the world's leading infectious cause of blindness, endemic in sub-Saharan Africa and parts of Asia and affecting millions of people. Serovars D-K cause genital tract infections and STIs. The organisms are the same species but different strains with different tissue tropisms and modes of transmission. The antibiotic treatments are the same.

faqs.question: Can chlamydia be transmitted through oral sex?
faqs.answer: Yes — chlamydia can be transmitted through oral sex, though pharyngeal (throat) chlamydia is less common than genital or rectal infection. Performing oral sex on a partner with a chlamydial urethral or vaginal infection can inoculate the pharynx with the bacteria. Pharyngeal chlamydia is usually asymptomatic and typically resolves spontaneously within weeks, but it can be treated with doxycycline. Receptive oral sex from an infected partner (fellatio) can also transmit chlamydia to the urethra. Using condoms or dental dams reduces this risk.

faqs.question: What is reactive arthritis from chlamydia?
faqs.answer: Reactive arthritis (formerly called Reiter syndrome) is a form of inflammatory arthritis that occurs in 1-3% of chlamydial infections, typically 1-4 weeks after the initial infection. It is characterized by the triad of arthritis (typically asymmetric oligoarthritis affecting the knees, ankles, and feet), urethritis (often preceding the arthritis), and conjunctivitis (red, irritated eyes). It is more common in men and is strongly associated with the HLA-B27 genetic marker. Reactive arthritis can become chronic and cause erosive joint damage. Treatment includes NSAIDs, corticosteroids, and disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) in addition to antibiotics for the chlamydial infection.

metaTitle: Chlamydia: Symptoms, Treatment, Causes & Prevention | aihealz
metaDescription: Chlamydia: most common bacterial STI. Learn symptoms, testing, treatment (doxycycline), complications (PID, infertility), partner treatment, and prevention. Reviewed by our medical team.

images.altText: Microscopic view of Chlamydia trachomatis inclusions inside host cells — the causative organism of chlamydia
images.caption: Chlamydia trachomatis is an obligate intracellular bacterium that replicates inside host cells, visible as intracytoplasmic inclusions under fluorescence microscopy.

images.altText: Diagram of the female reproductive system showing how chlamydia ascends from the cervix to cause pelvic inflammatory disease
images.caption: Untreated chlamydia ascends from the cervix through the uterus and fallopian tubes, causing PID and tubal scarring.

images.altText: Illustration showing correct condom use as a primary prevention method for chlamydia and other sexually transmitted infections
images.caption: Consistent and correct condom use reduces chlamydia transmission risk by approximately 90%.
